speaker: interviewer
text: It's the third tape. Thomas.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thomas, you said that they made you lie down in bed with a sick person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I hesitated. But there was no way out. And l laid down. And I still remember the terrible heat, because it was a-- a high fever. And it didn't take long, and I was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the person? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was-- with typhus, sick in typhus. So I did get sick. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Zellinger, the man who was taking care of me, he, of course, too, was taken as a sick person. But he was immediately taken to a better hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He has some influence in the Judenrat. I don't know, when I think about the hospital. But, to my surprise, a few days later, after I was in this hospital, I start receiving packages. When the doctor did come to visit me, he always had a little package for me, a few apples, a-- a cake-- a piece of cake, and so and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every day, I expected, about 4:00, when the doctor will come, he'll bring this package. Once, the doctor comes to visit us, and he made it fast. He didn't even come close to me. It's look-- it was looking like he was trying to avoid me. And while I see he was trying to get-- to go out, I asked him, Doctor, please. So he stopped. And I ask him, Zellinger didn't send me anything? And he said, Zellinger is dead. What's happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a similar set up in another hospital, where one room was for the prisoners. And just the day before, SS did come, took everybody out from this room and shot them. And they shot him, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, I was always afraid they will do-- why-- why-- why did they save us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it so happened that, one evening, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Jewish policeman had come in. Everybody in this room should go out and should go outside. Outside was a little yard. And across the yard was a prosektorium, a place where they keep the dead bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people kept-- stood up from the bed and they-- And it was January 8, 1943. This date I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- so I stood up, too. But I had a bad feeling, but I was very-- really, very sick. And I was moving slowly. And I remember, I went outside. And now, I don't remember why I did turn around and did go back. It could be two reasons. First of all, when I stepped, with my bare feet on the snow, it was like burning, like a hot iron. All because I notice, next to another little house outside, I notice SS soldiers. Anyway, I turn around, went back, and hide under the bed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About five minutes later, a Jewish policeman did come in. And I, laying under the bed, look for what I see. And I see this Jewish policeman. So I said, hello, hello, to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He noticed me. He said, ah, how did you stay? How did you-- did they let you stay here? And I told him, I don't know. And he said, OK, so don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hide. You will hear some shooting. But don't be scared. And so he left. And a few minutes later, I heard screaming, shooting from the-- from the nearby house. And it was quiet. The Jewish policeman didn't come back. But-- and I was under this bed. And when everything was quiet, I did move out and start to-- picked up my clothing from under the mattress to dress. But I realize that I could-- I couldn't move-- very shaky. But when I am in the street, I would probably drop down, because-- and they will recognize, immediately, I'm somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to go back under the bed. I was under the bed. And sometime later, I see the doctor comes in. When I see the doctor, I moved out. And when he did see me, it's like he would see a dead person. Ah, I can't believe it. How come? How come? So he start to check. What's happened is this, when you have a typhus, after 10 days, a-- a crisis will develop, when you jump from the bed, you want to run amok.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had, I remember, always, a couple cases when sick people, from the-- from this section, from the ghetto section, in this hospital, did want to run outside, through our room. But always, they were pushed back by the policemen. And there was a guy who recently-- the last couple of days, did try, practically, every hour to run out, and the police pushed him back. So exactly, his bed was empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the doctor discovered that there's a-- a empty bed in the civilian sections, so called. So he-- he understood what's happened, that when everybody moved, he picked up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody did push him back. There were no guards. And he went. Because the SS, they need 20 people, and there were 20 people. If there were 19, they would look for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the doctor said, OK, Toivi, go to this bed and forget about Blatt. From now on, you are Lederman. I forgot his first name, I think, David Lederman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the price of your life. If you forget, you're in trouble. And I will be in trouble. So I went to this bed. And later, I did get a crisis. And I remember-- and I remember that this bed, it looked to me dirty, the-- the-- the-- the cover and everything. So always, I was asking they should give me to another bed. And when they changed the beds, they changed the chart. And I remember some problems with the name, because when I have lost my mind, I said Lederman and sometimes not. Anyway, I remember some troubles there was, but everything was fine. And I was actually a free man. If I would be healthy, I-- I could out to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what's happened? The policeman, the Jewish policeman, did show up. And he told me, listen, you must be in the hospital. Why? Until you pay me money. He wanted about 1,000 zlotys or whatever. You can't go out. So I told him, where should take the money? I don't care. Steal. Without the money, you can't go out. So, as long as I was still sick, everything was fine. But later, the doctor said, OK, you're OK. You could go out from the hospital. So I told him, could I stay longer? No. I didn't want to tell him about the Jewish policeman, because it wasn't my doctor. It was another doctor. And I didn't want to tell him the truth. He didn't know about me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My doctor, I didn't see for a while. Otherwise, I would confide to him. Maybe he was-- I don't know. And-- and he said, no, you must go out. I signed you-- and he signed me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now I-- I didn't get food. I didn't have a bed anymore, because they might admit somebody else. So I was hiding in a little-- you know-- where they keep the brooms and everything. I was sleeping in the beds. I was stealing food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, after typhus, you're very hungry. I remember, at nighttime, I'd crawl to the people, who were practically dying, and open, quietly, there, wherever they keep the food. They had little stools.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was stealing just to keep me going. Until finally, one day, they caught me, the doctor. What are you doing here? You should be out. He pushed me out the door. So this was against the order from the Jewish policeman. So then, where should I go. And I remembered, there's not far another Jewish hospital. So I went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I was in this-- just open the door, from the other hospital, it was about a half a mile farther, a little clinic that still-- Jews were over there. And a Jew-- another Jewish policeman did jump in, start to beat me up. You, I will show you. This look like the-- my policeman, when he left duty, probably told another policeman, watch him. And-- so anyway, he was a strange fellow, with a Jewish cap, which identify him as a Jewish policeman. He beat me up and start to chase me back to the old hospital. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I was back. And-- and the worst thing was, that they're chasing me back, and, at that time, there was another bunch of Jews from the jail. So I was-- and he put me in this. So I was right where I started. Luckily, the Jewish doctor-- it looked like the Jewish doctor was taking care of the Jews from the jail. He did come in and see me over there. And he said, what are you doing here? So I didn't know what-- I told him the-- everything, that's happened up till now. And he-- and there was the commandant of the Jewish police or still a member, in the city of Stryj, was Mr. Stark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Stark was exactly in the hospital. And the doctor told me-- took me to the-- to the Jewish policeman. When I have seen the Jewish commandant, because they had, like, on their shoulder, they had the-- the like markings. And he said, Mr. Stark, to him. And I was aware that Mr. Stark is the commandant of the police. I was terribly scared. But Mr. Stark started to talk to me. And I told him what's happened. And he called in this militia, and he beat him up in my presence. He beat him up. And so-- and he told me, Tom, come with me to the ghetto. And I went to the ghetto. And in the ghetto, I didn't have it bad, because a lot of people were aware that I'm the one who survived from the jail and so on. I was never hungry. Where-- where-- it was a-- at a special-- there was a restaurant, which especially took care of me. Even the ghetto, where, you know, there were island of good-- of good restaurants and so on, cabarets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a-- a restaurant, where always they did-- they did feed me. And I had-- because there were a lot of empty houses, I had to find a room with blankets, with everything. And I was sleeping over there. Anyway. So this way, I was finally a free man. Tom, I-- I need to ask you something. Those 20 people, who were taken out, and you stayed under the bed, were they shot? Or were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were shot

speaker: interviewer
text: --let go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shot, of course. They were shot. The Nazis didn't want to go in-- in a-- a room contaminated by typhus. So they took them out to another room. So this way, I was a free man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a free man. The first thing what I've done? When I was still in hospital, I read-- I wrote a letter to Izbica, because I-- I-- I-- I find out, in Izbica, there's still people, Jews. The ghetto still exists, a small ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left about 200 Jews. There was a tannery producing leather. And the Germans liquidated Izbica, and they had a tannery. And later-- they've done to many towns. This was another kind of deception. When the ghettos were empty, they-- after a few months, the Germans realizing that a lot of Jews are still hiding someplace, after a few months, they declared the same ghetto, again, a judenstaat. The same it was with Izbica. They declared Izbica a judenstaat. In between time, my mother did come back from Warsaw. She was working as a bookkeeper in Warsaw, in a German company. My brother and my father did come back from their hiding place. Only I was in the still a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wrote a letter, from the hospital, that I'm here. Please, send me money. There's no answer. Now, I'm out from the ghetto-- out from the jail. I'm in the ghetto. I write another letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: No answer. So I thought, I must escape from the ghetto. Because any day, they will take us to Belzec or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the Jewish-- to the Judenrat. And I told him, mister, could you help me some way. I-- I need a few zlotys. I'm hungry. So he went to this official-- went to another room. It didn't take him long. He comes back with a paper that I'm allowed to go back to the hospital, as-- and I could be fed over there for the next two weeks as a reconvalecent. A convalescent.

speaker: interviewee
text: A convalescent. For two weeks, I'm allowed to eat in the hospital. So I took this paper. I was disappointed that he didn't give me money. I went back. And I never went to this hospital. First of all, up till now, I am a late sleeper. Don't call me to-- to meet before 9 o'clock. So I never did go out. And again, I can't understand it. And I-- there must be something. I-- I'm agnostic. But there must be something. I have a strong intuition, though. Because, as I said, I am a late sleeper. But this day, I waked up about 6 o'clock in the morning and told myself, I'm going to the hospital to try the food, to eat there. And I went out. Now the rule was-- and at that time, the ghetto was smaller, and the hospital was sort of outside the ghetto fences. You could go to this hospital only when a-- a policeman, a Jewish policeman was with you. And his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were-- have you been sleeping in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there were so many empty houses, I just picked up a room in a empty house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went. I didn't even-- I was so impatient, I didn't even wait for the policeman. There was a hole in the fence. I went through and then to this hospital. I come to the hospital, wander around, show the paper, had a bite. And I was looking for my doctor, you know, to say hello to him. I go into the office, look around. I go out in the hallway. And there's my doctor, running, looking for me. He said-- I-- he was in the office. I didn't notice him before.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, are you Blatt? He didn't even recognize me for some reason. I say, yes. Go to the office, a woman is waiting for you. I go to the office, and there's a lady sitting over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I introduce myself, Blatt. And she said, I'm Ola. Ola did ring the bell. I had a nanny, her name was Ola. And I remember, the name was from childhood, growing with me. You know, I don't know if it's a nanny. It's-- probably, my mother didn't have enough milk or whatever. So she was-- she was-- and I remember, for years, Nanny, Nanny, Ola, Ola.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, are you Toivi? I say, yes. Could you tell me how your father look like? Could you tell me where you lived? Like an interrogation. And I told everything Izbica look like, and my father and my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, she was convinced. And she told me, I'm Ola, your nanny, former nanny. A letter did come, from the hospital, that you're over there, and you asked for money. So your dad send a letter to the hospital. Part of the-- my mistake was I didn't say, you should be writing Lederman. And they received a telegram, Thomas Blatt died, January 8th, in the Jewish Hospital, in the city of Stryj. But later, another letter had come from the ghetto. And he was confused. And he was sure that somebody want to get some money. But my brother-- my brother, my little brother said, no, Toivi is alive. You must.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as a last resort, just to-- just to please my brother, they asked my nanny to come interrogate. And she find out. I remember, she did give me a few zlotys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, she had given me a watch, which was my brother's biggest treasure. So my brother did believe that I'm alive. She gave me this watch. And she left. This watch didn't last long, because, when I went back in the ghetto, I met a Jewish policeman, who noticed, immediately, the watch and took it, [LAUGHS] immediately. Anyway, he actually did want to denounce me to the Nazis if I didn't give him more money. But I convinced him that I don't have-- anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Soon, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a few weeks later, a knock on the door. Who comes in? Mr. Kraus, a Nazi from Izbica, a Volksdeutscher, ethnic German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Toivi-- he recognized me immediately. And I was a little bit scared. What's the-- Toivi, your dad sent me here. You have a permit to travel with the train, from the Landrat. Landrat is also like a German administration office from the city of Krasnystaw. Probably, they were paid very much. They were bribed-- paid much money from my father. And we are leaving, today, back to Izbica. I have a permit. He had a permit, a paper. So he took me. It didn't-- it take maybe 15 minutes. He took me out. He go to the ghetto. They let me through, because he's a Nazi. And we go to the train station. It was sort of the dark. He told me to wait. He will go and buy a ticket. He comes back in five minutes. Toivi, he said, listen. We could go with the regular train. But if you will have trouble-- there are a lot of Ukrainians. You know how they are. If you will have trouble, I can't really protect you much. But there's a train, an army train, nur fur Deutsche, only for Germans, leaving to Warsaw, with wounded soldiers from the Eastern Front. So come in over there. And I'll tell you, it's the safest place in the lion's den. In that time, I never had a-- a armband. But while I had the permit, so I put, in the ghetto, the armband. Otherwise, I never wear it, even in Izbica.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took down the armband, went with him to this train, went to a compartment. There was a woman, some kind of officer, I think, from Tot organization. There was a SS man sitting, gendarme. And I-- and I was miserable looking. You know, from the jail, skin's not heal.

speaker: interviewee
text: He introduced himself. And this is my-- his nephew. And I-- he just takes me from the hospital. He did also warn me, I shouldn't talk at all. Anyway, I remember the-- the Nazi, the German, did put himself vodka. And he was joking. He wasn't really to give me. He say, drink. And the woman pat him on the hand. She did give me tea. And I thought, God, if they only find out I'm Jewish. And I said, danke schoen. And I did see his look, the Kraus' look. I was quiet. I didn't say anything else. And I remember, again, passing Belzec. And again, the smell. And I thought, God, so many months later and still burning. Can still-- and that time, Belzec didn't function anymore. But cremation did go on from the graves. They opened the graves. But it was Belzec functioned till the end of 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: I finally arrive to Izbica. I go down-- go to our home. He forgot to tell me, Kraus, that we are moved to another place. I knock on the door. And I hear a voice. Who is it? And I didn't recognize him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, I said, it's me, Toivi. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH], you dirty Jew, run away. Get out of here. You don't live her anymore. Please, tell me where-- where we live. Oh, in Schneidmesser's house. Schneidmesser, the same place, where I used to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over there. There were shutters. And I put my fingers behind the shutters and knock on the window. And the light was still on. And I hear a voice. Who is it? And I say, me, open. And I hear, immediately, my brother is yelling, oh, Toivi, Toivi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they open the door. And I go in. And my father was pretending he doesn't cry. I-- He turned his face. And my mother-- and I was most happy to see my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we talked, a whole night, practically. I had here-- because, in the Jewish part-- portion of the jail, I was sleeping on the floor. So I had here, you know, like you have a wound. You have, you know? It was very bad, on both sides. Because I was sleeping on the-- a hard floor. So she looked at this, my mother. And later, she takes an envelope and handed-- and my picture, with a black piece of a-- fabric. And it-- and she told me to read the piece of paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a telegraph from the Jewish Judenrat, in-- from-- from the hospital. And there's a time there. This is the reason I remember January 8th. And my dad, again, was superstitious. He said, if somebody said you die, you are dead. When you're alive, you will survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyways, to make a long story short, because I know time is going on, April 28-- Maybe I should tell this, too. So the first day I go out to Izbica, my Izbica, everybody knows me. Happy. It was a sunny day. And the first greeting was the janitor from the Polish Christian communal store. I-- I said-- I said, dzien dobry, good morning, Mr. Franciszek. He said, whoa, good morning. You still alive? I hope not for too long. I was so shocked. This was my first greeting in Izbica.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went through Izbica. And I have seen the houses taken apart by Poles looking for hidden treasures. You know how it was. And come back home, I was disappointed. And we are all-- we're living practically in that one section, one place, as I said, the tannery. And suddenly-- and we were aware that this is our last days.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought I should buy a gun. When I mentioned to my father, I want money, he didn't give me. So I find another friend, Gdanski, Ryszek Gdanski. He was a-- a-- the-- he was resettled from the city of Poznan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, Ryszek, your father is a watchmaker. He probably could get someone to let buy a gun. So he added some money. And I told my father that I want to sleep on the attic because too many people in the room. And I need a blanket. So he did give me a blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sold the blanket. And he bought a little gun from a Jewish fellow. Pele was his name. Bought it with a blanket. And I used to hide this pistol where the-- you know, in the old tanneries, there weren't electrical motors.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a horse, which was-- his face-- his eyes were tied. And he did run, day and night, all around, turning the-- the engine, the-- the-- the-- or wheels, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and there was a haystack, for-- for food for this horse. So I hide it in the haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: April 28, while sleeping, I hear a rifle shot. And I understood that something terrible happened. I look out the window, through the attic, and I see the soldiers, Nazi soldiers, Ukrainians coming. And it didn't take long. They smashed the gate from the tannery. They did come in. But most of us were hidden already. We had special hiding places. As I mentioned, Izbica was in a valley. All around were hills. And so the-- the tannery was built in on one side of the hill. So we took out a panel and digged a big place in the hill. And later, the panel was so very nicely closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- and they looked. And they left, the Nazis. They didn't find us. And we could hear every word. And finally, we heard voices in Polish. They must be here. We have seen them yesterday. They didn't escape. They're hiding something. They must be here. And the Germans did come back. And finally, I remember, they were pulling the panels out. And, when they were pulling the panel, light did come in into our hiding place. And they were flashing. The light was flashing. And I understood that, just at the entrance, finally, the panel cracked, and they find the opening. And they told us to go out. I remember, I went out, but I left a shoe inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember, I was afraid, if they take us to a concentration camp with one-- with one shoe, I-- it could be trouble. So I remember going to this Nazi and asking him if I could go down, pick up my--

speaker: interviewee
text: He allowed me to pick up the shoe. I picked up the shoe, went out. And they took us outside the tannery. And they were-- we were surrounded by a couple of Nazis. And Polish people were all around, looking. And this time, again, I managed, actually, to go out from this encirclement and mingle with the people. I was barefoot. I was looking like a Pole. And at that time, I was rather-- rather blond, reddish-blond, lighter hair. The nose was short, a little bit. You know, you get old, that nose is growing. So I didn't look typical Jewish. I mingled. And now I know I must hide, because, any minute, somebody will denounce me or whatever. So I notice a-- a-- a schoolmate of mine, Janek Knopczek. And I told him, Janek, hide me. Please, help me. He said, of course. He was many times in our home. He eat in our place. He slept even sometimes. So I went to-- he did live nearby. So he said, go to the barn. I go to the barn. There's a big padlock on the barn. So I can't go in. So I'm looking how to go into the barn to hide, until everything is over, so I could escape to the forest or wherever. I see a woman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a older woman. No, she might-- maybe she was 23, but you know, when you're 15, everything above 17 is old! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And she start to yell, Blatt, run, run, run. So I said, what's happened? Janek is coming. If she would tell me a Nazi is coming, I would run. But Janek? He will open the-- the padlock and let me in. So-- but why is she so panicky? It didn't take long, I find out. Janek did come with a Nazi. And said to the Nazi, take him, he's a Jew. And I said, Janek, you're joking. Tell me you're joking. No, he's a Jew. And the Nazi took me. And Janek said, goodbye. The way he said it? He said, goodbye, Toivi, I will see you in a shelf in a soap store-- on a shelf in a soap store. Why on a shelf in a soap store? Because in that time, there were rumors that they're making soap from human bodies. So he will see me in a soap.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left. And I-- they took me to the marketplace, where all the Jews were assembled. And there, I see my-- my parents and my brother.
